entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been busier than usual. People going in and out all day.
details: I don't know if that means business is good or everyone's just restless.
Either way, it's nice to see some activity. Beats the place sitting empty.
reactions: Keeps the settlement feeling alive, at least.

statCheck.pass: I think it's the seasonal shift. People stock up around this time every year, and the {poi_type} is where they go first.
statCheck.fail: No idea what's driving it. Just more people than usual.

entries.intro: Good {resource_type} coming out of the {poi_type} these days. Better than it's been in a while.
details: The quality's been steady all season. People are noticing.
Keeps everyone in work, which keeps everyone in a decent mood.
reactions: I hope they can keep it up. A good run like this makes the lean stretches easier to swallow.

statCheck.pass: The vein they're working is deeper than the last one. Richer material down there, takes more effort to get at it but the yield is worth it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the details. I just know what I see coming out.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} hasn't been pulling its weight lately. You can feel it around town.
details: Output's down and people are starting to worry. Not panicking, but the mood's shifted.
It's the kind of slowdown that creeps up on you. One week it's fine, the next you're counting what's left.
reactions: These things come and go. Hopefully it picks back up before anyone gets desperate.

statCheck.pass: The equipment's old, half the tools need replacing and nobody's budgeted for it. That's the real bottleneck.
statCheck.fail: Couldn't tell you why. I just know things aren't what they were.

entries.intro: Walked past the {poi_type} yesterday and there was nobody around. Completely empty.
details: Usually there's at least someone there. Felt strange seeing it quiet like that.
I checked back later and things were normal again. Must have just hit an odd hour.
reactions: Probably nothing. But you notice when a familiar place feels different.

statCheck.pass: The shift patterns changed recently. There's a gap in the middle of the day now where nobody's assigned.
statCheck.fail: I don't keep track of who works where. Just seemed odd.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has been pulling in good {resource_type} lately. Better than usual, actually.
details: I don't know if it's the season or if someone figured out a better method.
Either way, people seem less stressed about supplies. Makes a difference in the mood around here.
reactions: When the basics are covered, everything else gets a little easier.

entries.intro: Something needs doing about the path to the {poi_type}. It's been getting worse all season.
details: Tree roots have buckled the stones and nobody's relaid them. You have to watch every step.
I nearly turned my ankle there last week carrying a load. Not ideal.
reactions: It's one of those jobs that keeps getting put off because it's not urgent. Until someone falls.

statCheck.pass: Cutting back the two biggest roots and repacking the base would hold for another few years. Half a day's work at most.
statCheck.fail: I wouldn't know where to start with stonework. I just know the path is bad.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} smells different depending on the season. Right now it's that sharp, earthy smell: almost metallic.
details: In summer it's drier, more like dust and warm stone. In the wet months the whole area has this rich, heavy scent.
I've been walking past it for years and I still notice it every time. Some smells you don't get used to.
reactions: It's one of those things that tells you where you are without looking.

statCheck.pass: That metallic scent usually means {resource_type} deposits are close to the surface. Moisture brings the mineral smell out.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what causes the smell. It just is what it is.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} used to look different. The trees around it were thicker, the path less worn.
details: More people passing through each year wears a place down. Wider trails, fewer wildflowers, more bare dirt.
I remember when you had to push through brush to get there. Now you could drive a cart through.
reactions: It's not ruined or anything, just different. Places change the same as people.

statCheck.pass: Foot traffic compacts the soil and kills the ground cover. Once the roots go, erosion does the rest faster than you'd think.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it's changed so much. Time, I guess.

entries.intro: If you stand near the {poi_type} at dusk, you can hear everything. Water, birds, the wind through the stones.
details: During the day there's too much going on to notice. But in the evening the place has its own sound.
I went out there once just to listen and ended up staying until it was fully dark. Lost track of time.
reactions: Not everywhere has that. Some places are just noisier than they are anything else.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks different in the rain: quieter, almost peaceful if you don't mind getting soaked.
details: Most people avoid it in bad weather. I like going when nobody's there.
The stone darkens when it's wet and the sound of rain on the roof makes the whole place feel smaller.
reactions: I don't work there. I just like how it feels when it's empty.

entries.intro: I've never worked at the {poi_type} but I walk past it every day. It's become part of my routine.
details: I know the sounds it makes at different times of day: clanging in the morning, quiet by afternoon.
The people who work there wave at me now. I think they think I'm inspecting something.
reactions: It's just on my way. But I'd notice if it were gone.

statCheck.pass: Places take on rhythm from the people who use them. You can read a lot about a trade just by listening to its workspace.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what half the sounds mean. I just know the schedule.

entries.intro: The {crop_type} near the {poi_type} is coming in well this season. You can see it from the road.
details: The rows are straight and the color is good. Whoever's tending it knows what they're doing.
I tried growing {crop_type} once and gave up after the first year. It's harder than it looks.
reactions: I'll stick to what I'm good at and buy theirs.

statCheck.pass: That soil near the {poi_type} drains well and gets good sun. Perfect conditions for {crop_type} if you time the planting right.
statCheck.fail: I don't know much about farming. It looks healthy to me, but what do I know.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} always has a line of people outside first thing in the morning. Every single day.
details: You'd think they'd spread out, but no. Everyone wants to be first.
I stopped trying to beat the rush. I just go after lunch when it's quiet.
reactions: It's not worth the headache. Let the early risers fight over it.

entries.intro: Someone left a cart outside the {poi_type} for three days. Just sitting there blocking the way.
details: Nobody claimed it, nobody moved it. We all just walked around it like it belonged there.
It had mud on the wheels and nothing in the bed. Just an empty cart, taking up space.
reactions: Eventually it disappeared. I still don't know whose it was.

entries.intro: I like the {poi_type} best in winter. The smoke from the chimney, the frost on the stones.
details: Everything looks sharper when it's cold. The colors stand out more against the grey.
People move quicker in winter too. Less standing around chatting, more getting things done.
reactions: Summer's fine, but winter gives a place character.

entries.intro: There's a dog that hangs around the {poi_type}. Nobody owns it, but everybody feeds it.
details: It knows exactly when people take breaks. Shows up right on time, every time.
I think it sleeps under the eaves. I've seen it curled up there in the evenings.
reactions: Honestly, that dog has a better schedule than I do.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} needs a new roof. You can see the sag from the road.
details: It's been like that for months. Every rainstorm I expect the whole thing to give.
Nobody wants to pay for it, and nobody wants to be the one to bring it up at a meeting.
reactions: It'll get fixed when it collapses. That's how things work around here.

statCheck.pass: The main beam is bowing under the load. If they sister a new timber alongside it, the rest of the structure is still sound.
statCheck.fail: I'm not a roofer. I just know it looks bad.

entries.intro: You ever notice how loud the {poi_type} gets around midday? You can hear it from three streets over.
details: Hammering, shouting, carts moving. The whole area just comes alive.
It quiets down by evening, but for a few hours it's the loudest place in town.
reactions: I don't mind it: a working sound means things are happening.

entries.intro: The ground around the {poi_type} is always muddy. Even in dry weather there's a soft patch by the entrance.
details: I think there's a spring underneath, or a pipe that leaks. Something's feeding water into that spot.
People track it everywhere. You can follow the muddy footprints halfway across town.
reactions: Someone should dig a channel or lay some gravel. It's been like this for years.

statCheck.pass: There's definitely groundwater seeping up through the foundation. The soil there is clay, so it holds every drop.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it's wet. It just always is.

entries.intro: I brought some {resource_type} home from the {poi_type} last week. Best batch they've had in ages.
details: Clean, good weight, no waste. You could tell just by looking at it.
I don't usually get excited about {resource_type}, but this lot was something else.
reactions: Makes you appreciate the people who do the actual work.

entries.intro: {npc_name} works at the {poi_type} and always looks exhausted. I worry about that one.
details: They're there before dawn and I still see them coming home after dark.
I offered to help once and they just waved me off. Too proud or too tired to accept.
reactions: There's a difference between hard work and running yourself into the ground.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has been running smoothly. No complaints from anyone, which is rare.
details: Usually somebody has a gripe about output or hours or quality. But lately, nothing.
I think the crew there finally found a rhythm that works.
reactions: When nobody's complaining, that's the highest praise you'll get around here.

entries.intro: I keep meaning to stop by the {poi_type} and I keep putting it off. No real reason.
details: It's not far. I just never get around to it before the day runs out.
I walk past the turnoff every morning and think 'today's the day.' Then I keep walking.
reactions: It's become a running joke with myself at this point. Tomorrow, always tomorrow.

entries.intro: There's a crack in the wall at the {poi_type}. I noticed it last week and it's definitely bigger now.
details: It runs from the foundation up to the window. You can fit a finger in it.
I mentioned it to someone and they shrugged. Said it's been there forever.
reactions: Forever or not, cracks don't stop growing on their own.

statCheck.pass: That's a settling crack: the foundation on the east side is probably on softer ground than the west. Fixable, but not cheap.
statCheck.fail: I don't know much about masonry. It just looks worrying to me.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} smells like woodsmoke today. They must be burning something different.
details: Usually it's a sharper smell, more like pine. This is heavier, almost sweet.
It clings to your clothes if you stand near the door. I caught a whiff of it on my sleeve hours later.
reactions: You start to read a place by its smoke after a while. Each season has its own scent.

statCheck.pass: That's applewood or cherry: burns slower and cooler than pine. Better for steady work, worse for quick heat.
statCheck.fail: I couldn't tell you what wood it is. Just that it smells different.

entries.intro: I saw kids playing near the {poi_type} again. They're always climbing on things they shouldn't.
details: One of them was up on the fence post pretending to be a knight. Charming until someone falls.
The workers don't seem to mind. I think they like the company.
reactions: We all did the same thing at that age. You just worry more when you're the adult watching.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had a good week. You can tell by the mood of the people coming out of there.
details: Smiles, whistling, nobody dragging their feet. That only happens when output is up.
Even {npc_name} seemed cheerful, and they're usually the grumpy one.
reactions: A good week at the {poi_type} means a good week for the whole settlement.

entries.intro: The lantern outside the {poi_type} has been out for days. Makes that whole stretch dark at night.
details: I nearly tripped over a barrel walking past in the dark. No warning, just suddenly on the ground.
It's an easy fix. Someone just needs to bother doing it.
reactions: I'd fix it myself if I could reach the bracket.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks good from the hill to the {direction}. You can see the whole layout.
details: It fits into the landscape well. Like it grew there instead of being built.
I go up there sometimes just to see the settlement from above. The {poi_type} always catches my eye first.
reactions: Whoever chose that spot knew what they were doing.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} gets cold drafts something terrible. You can feel the wind cutting through the walls.
details: In summer it's actually pleasant, keeps the place cool. But winter is miserable.
The workers stuff rags in the gaps every year and every year the wind pulls them out.
reactions: Glad I don't have to spend my days in there.

statCheck.pass: The mortar between the lower stones has crumbled. Cold air follows the path of least resistance, and those gaps are getting wider each freeze cycle.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it's so drafty. Old buildings just are, I suppose.

entries.intro: I've started timing my walks to go past the {poi_type} in the late afternoon. The light is perfect then.
details: The sun hits the stonework at just the right angle and the whole place glows warm.
It only lasts about half an hour before the shadow of the ridge swallows it.
reactions: Small pleasures. You take them where you find them.

entries.intro: Somebody painted the {poi_type}'s door. Bright red, out of nowhere.
details: Nobody asked for it, nobody voted on it. Just showed up one morning.
Half the people love it, the other half want it back the way it was.
reactions: I think it looks fine, but I'm staying out of that argument.

entries.intro: The well water near the {poi_type} has tasted off this week. Not bad, just different.
details: A bit more mineral, maybe. Like sucking on a stone.
Nobody's gotten sick or anything. It's just not the same as usual.
reactions: Probably just seasonal runoff changing the groundwater. Happens every year.

statCheck.pass: Heavy rain upstream shifts the mineral content. Iron-rich soil seeps into the aquifer and you taste it for a week or two until it flushes.
statCheck.fail: I'm not a well-digger. Could be anything.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the first place I check when I need to know what kind of day it's going to be.
details: If it's busy and noisy, it's a good day. If it's quiet and the door's shut, something's off.
I've done it so long that the day feels wrong if I don't pass by. Like I missed something important.
reactions: I don't even think about it anymore. I just look and I know.

entries.intro: The {resource_type} from the {poi_type} hasn't been great lately. Thinner than it should be.
details: People are making do, but you can tell it's not up to the usual standard.
A few people have started going to other sources for their {resource_type}. That's never a good sign.
reactions: Hopefully it's a rough patch and not a trend.

statCheck.pass: The top layer's been worked too hard. They need to go deeper or shift to a new section, but that takes time and labor nobody has spare.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the details. I just know what I'm getting isn't as good.

entries.intro: The flowers near the {poi_type} are blooming. Whole patch of them, bright purple and yellow.
details: I don't think anyone planted them. They just showed up one spring and kept coming back.
The bees love them. You can hear the buzzing from the path.
reactions: Nice to have something pretty next to a working place.

entries.intro: I can always tell when the {poi_type} is shutting down for the day. The sounds change.
details: Tools being put away, doors closing, the clatter fading out. It's a specific rhythm.
The last worker out always checks the latch twice. I've watched them do it a hundred times.
reactions: There's something satisfying about the end-of-day quiet.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} draws a lot of traffic from the {direction}. More than it used to.
details: I keep seeing unfamiliar faces heading that way. Not travelers exactly, just people from neighboring areas.
Word must have gotten around about the quality of what they're producing.
reactions: Good for business. Less good for the path, which is taking a beating.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s chimney was smoking funny this morning. Thick and dark, not the usual wisp.
details: I watched it for a while to make sure nothing was actually wrong. It cleared up eventually.
Probably just damp fuel. Still makes your heart jump for a second.
reactions: Fire is the one thing nobody takes lightly around here.

statCheck.pass: Damp wood produces incomplete combustion: more soot, more creosote. If the chimney hasn't been swept this season, that's a real risk.
statCheck.fail: I don't know much about chimneys. I just know dark smoke is usually bad.

entries.intro: I helped carry supplies to the {poi_type} last week. Heavier load than I expected.
details: They're stocking up for something. More {resource_type} than usual, stacked to the ceiling.
Nobody explained why, and I didn't ask. I just carried what they pointed at.
reactions: Whatever they're preparing for, they're serious about it. That's either reassuring or worrying.

entries.intro: There's a tree next to the {poi_type} that's been leaning more each year. One good storm and it's coming down.
details: The roots are half out of the ground on one side. You can see where the soil has washed away.
If it falls toward the building, that's a real problem.
reactions: Better to take it down on purpose than wait for nature to decide when.

statCheck.pass: The root ball is compromised on the downhill side. A controlled fell with two guide ropes would drop it safely away from the structure.
statCheck.fail: I'm no woodsman. I just know which way it's leaning.

entries.intro: {npc_name} told me the {poi_type} is producing more than ever. Said it with a grin, too.
details: I don't usually take boasting at face value, but the evidence is hard to argue with.
There's more {resource_type} coming through town than I've seen in a long while.
reactions: Good for them. And good for the rest of us.

entries.intro: I stood outside the {poi_type} this morning and just listened. There's a rhythm to it.
details: Clang, pause, clang, scrape, over and over. It's almost musical if you're in the right mood.
I think the workers don't even notice it anymore. It's just the sound of their day.
reactions: Every trade has its own beat. You just have to stand still long enough to hear it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} was shut for two days last week. Nobody told us why.
details: People were milling around with nothing to do. You don't realize how much depends on that place until it stops.
When it reopened, everything went back to normal like nothing happened.
reactions: I'd just like to know when these things are planned. A little warning goes a long way.

entries.intro: There's a spot behind the {poi_type} where you can sit and nobody bothers you. I go there sometimes.
details: Just a flat stone against the back wall. Gets sun in the afternoon.
The workers know I sit there and they leave me be. It's an understanding.
reactions: Everyone needs a quiet spot. That one's mine.

entries.intro: I think the {poi_type} could use another pair of hands. The people there are stretched thin.
details: They're doing the work of five with three. You can see it in how tired they look by midweek.
Nobody's volunteering, though. Everyone's got their own work to worry about.
reactions: It's not sustainable. Something's going to give eventually.

entries.intro: The morning fog sits heavy around the {poi_type}. Makes the whole place look like it's floating.
details: You can barely see the building, just the outline and the glow of lanterns inside.
By midmorning it burns off and it's just a building again. But for an hour it's something else.
reactions: I'm a sucker for a good fog. Makes everything feel like a story.

entries.intro: The road to the {poi_type} from the {direction} has gotten wider. More ruts, more wear.
details: Heavy carts do that. The edges crumble and the center dips until you've got a trench.
Last rain it was ankle-deep in mud. I had to go around through the grass.
reactions: Roads don't fix themselves. Someone needs to lay some stone before winter.

statCheck.pass: The drainage is the real issue. A shallow ditch on either side would channel the water and keep the surface from turning to soup every time it rains.
statCheck.fail: I don't know road building. I just know it's getting worse.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the best-maintained building in town and it's not even close.
details: Clean walls, solid roof, tidy surroundings. Whoever runs it takes pride in the place.
I wish some of the houses looked half as good.
reactions: Sets a standard, whether people want to admit it or not.

entries.intro: I traded some {resource_type} at the {poi_type} yesterday. Fair deal, no haggling.
details: That's how it should be. You know what things are worth, they know what things are worth.
I've had worse dealings at bigger markets.
reactions: Honest work for honest pay. Can't ask for more than that.

entries.intro: I heard the {poi_type} is running low on {resource_type}. Not out, but getting there.
details: People start hoarding when they hear that. Makes the problem worse.
I'm trying not to worry about it. These things usually sort themselves out.
reactions: Panic is always worse than the actual shortage.

statCheck.pass: The supply line from the {direction} has been slow. A single delayed shipment can ripple through the whole stock if there's no buffer built in.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the logistics. I just heard what I heard.

entries.intro: The shade near the {poi_type} is the best in town during summer. Big old tree just blocks the worst of it.
details: People gather there during the hottest part of the day. Almost like an unofficial meeting spot.
The roots have heaved the path a bit, but nobody wants to cut the tree for that.
reactions: Some things are worth a bumpy path.

entries.intro: The noise from the {poi_type} kept me up last night. They were working late, way past sundown.
details: I don't know what was so urgent. Banging and scraping until I gave up trying to sleep.
My window faces right that way, so I get the full concert.
reactions: I'll survive. But I wish they'd warn people when they plan to go late.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} always smells like work. Sweat, dust, warm metal, whatever they're burning that day.
details: It's not pleasant exactly, but it's honest. You walk past and you know things are getting done.
My clothes pick up the smell if I stand there too long.
reactions: I'd rather smell work than nothing at all.

entries.intro: I watched them unloading {resource_type} at the {poi_type} this morning. Must have been a big haul.
details: Cart after cart, and the workers were grinning. That's always a good sign.
They had to stack {resource_type} outside because the storage was already full. Good problem to have.
reactions: The settlement eats when the {poi_type} produces. Simple as that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a weathervane on top that squeaks in the wind. Drives me up the wall.
details: On a still day it's fine. But any breeze at all and it starts up: eee, eee, eee, all day long.
I mentioned it once and was told it's been squeaking since before I was born.
reactions: A single drop of oil: that's all it would take.

entries.intro: The sunset behind the {poi_type} last night was spectacular. Orange and pink all along the ridge.
details: The silhouette of the building against that sky was something you'd want to paint.
I stood there like an idiot just staring until the color faded.
reactions: Doesn't cost anything to look up once in a while.

entries.intro: {npc_name} has been spending a lot of time at the {poi_type}. More than their share, I'd say.
details: I see them going in early and coming out late. Not sure if they're dedicated or avoiding something.
The others there don't seem to mind. Might even be grateful for the extra help.
reactions: None of my business, really. Just something I've noticed.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks lonely at night. Just a dark shape against the sky with no lights.
details: During the day it's full of life, but after hours it's like a different place entirely.
I walked past it once after midnight and it felt like the building was watching me.
reactions: Buildings don't have moods. But try telling yourself that in the dark.

entries.intro: Rain collects on the {poi_type}'s roof and pours off in one spot. Made a little gully in the ground.
details: There's no gutter on that side, so all the water channels to the low point and dumps.
In a heavy rain it's like a waterfall: kids love it, but the foundation probably doesn't.
reactions: Another thing on the list nobody's getting to.

statCheck.pass: That concentrated water flow is undermining the footing. A simple rain barrel or splash block would redirect it before it does real damage.
statCheck.fail: I don't know building stuff. Just seems like a lot of water in one spot.

entries.intro: I bumped into {npc_name} coming out of the {poi_type}. They seemed pleased with themselves.
details: Wouldn't say what about. Just had that look, like they knew something good that I didn't.
I'll probably find out in a few days. News doesn't stay quiet long around here.
reactions: Good for them. I could use a day like that myself.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the heart of this place, whether people admit it or not.
details: Take it away and half the settlement wouldn't know what to do with themselves.
It's where people meet, where work happens, where news spreads. Everything flows through it.
reactions: You don't think about it until you imagine it gone. Then you realize.

entries.intro: The fence around the {poi_type} is falling apart. Posts leaning, boards missing.
details: It's more decoration than barrier at this point. A stiff breeze could flatten it.
I think the last repair was years ago. You can see where someone patched it with mismatched wood.
reactions: At some point patching stops making sense and you just need to start over.

statCheck.pass: The posts are rotting at ground level where moisture sits. Replacing them with stone footings would add twenty years to the life of the fence.
statCheck.fail: I'm no carpenter. It just looks rough.

entries.intro: Mornings at the {poi_type} are the best time to visit. Everyone's fresh, nobody's grumpy yet.
details: There's energy in the air. People actually say hello instead of grunting.
By afternoon the mood shifts. Better to get your business done early.
reactions: I try to handle anything {poi_type}-related before noon. Life's easier that way.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} put out a bad batch of {resource_type} last week. People weren't happy.
details: It wasn't dangerous or anything, just poor quality. Below what anyone expected.
The crew there looked embarrassed. You could tell they knew.
reactions: Everyone has an off week. I just hope it's not a pattern.

statCheck.pass: The raw material was fine. I think the processing was rushed: they tried to push through too much volume and cut corners on the finishing.
statCheck.fail: I wouldn't know good technique from bad. I just know the result wasn't up to scratch.

entries.intro: There's a bench near the {poi_type} where the old-timers sit. They've been doing it for as long as anyone remembers.
details: Same spot every day, watching people come and go. They know everyone's business.
If you want to know what's really happening in this settlement, sit on that bench for an hour.
reactions: I'll be on that bench someday. It's not the worst retirement plan.

entries.intro: The wind funnels through the gap between the {poi_type} and the building next to it. Howls like a wolf.
details: On a calm day you'd never know. But when the wind picks up from the {direction}, the whole area moans.
First time I heard it I thought something was wrong. Now I barely notice.
reactions: Visitors always ask about it. We've all stopped hearing it.

statCheck.pass: The gap is just the right width to accelerate the airflow: wider wouldn't do it, narrower would block it. An accident of building placement.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it does that. Something about the shape of the gap.

entries.intro: I brought visitors past the {poi_type} last month. They were impressed.
details: Said their settlement doesn't have anything like it. Made me see the place with fresh eyes.
You stop noticing what you see every day until someone else points it out.
reactions: We do all right here. Easy to forget that sometimes.

entries.intro: Dust kicks up around the {poi_type} in dry weather. Gets in your eyes, your clothes, everything.
details: The ground there is all loose grit. No grass to hold it down.
By the end of a dry spell everything within fifty paces has a layer of dust on it.
reactions: I try to avoid walking that way when it hasn't rained. Not always possible.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} echoes at night. Sounds carry differently when nobody's working.
details: A loose shutter banging, something settling in the walls. Normal stuff that sounds eerie after dark.
I've heard people say the place is haunted, but it's not. Old buildings just creak.
reactions: Everything sounds bigger in the quiet. You hear things at night you'd never notice by day.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} got new tools recently. You can tell by the way the work sounds different.
details: Cleaner strikes, less scraping. The old equipment must have been in worse shape than anyone admitted.
Morale seems up too. People work better when their tools work properly.
reactions: Should have done it sooner, honestly.

statCheck.pass: Good tools reduce waste and fatigue. A sharp blade does the work of three dull ones, and the worker goes home with energy to spare.
statCheck.fail: I don't use that kind of equipment. I just know new sounds better than old.

entries.intro: There's always a pile of waste material outside the {poi_type}. It grows all week and vanishes on rest day.
details: Offcuts, shavings, broken bits. The usual byproducts of work.
I've seen people pick through it for useful scraps before the cleanup crew comes.
reactions: One person's waste is another's project material. That's just how it works.

entries.intro: I avoid the {poi_type} at lunchtime. Everyone crowds the entrance and you can't get through.
details: Workers spill out with their meals and suddenly the path is a dining hall.
They're perfectly friendly about it, but navigating around twenty people eating is a chore.
reactions: I just take a different route. Easier than asking people to move.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has held up well through the years. Sturdy construction, that.
details: The walls are still plumb, the doors still hang true. Whoever built it knew their trade.
Most buildings around here show their age. The {poi_type} wears it well.
reactions: Good bones make all the difference.

statCheck.pass: The foundation is set on bedrock, and the timber framing uses mortise-and-tenon joints throughout. That's old-school craftsmanship: built to last generations.
statCheck.fail: I don't know construction. It just seems solid to me.

entries.intro: The {crop_type} field near the {poi_type} has been struggling. Yellowing at the tips.
details: It looked fine a few weeks ago. Something changed and now the whole plot looks tired.
The person tending it has been out there every day, but you can only do so much.
reactions: Farming is a gamble. Sometimes the land just doesn't cooperate.

statCheck.pass: Yellowing tips usually means a nitrogen deficiency. The soil's been planted too many seasons without a fallow year, and it's exhausted.
statCheck.fail: I know nothing about crops. It just looks off.

entries.intro: I can smell the {poi_type} before I see it most days. It's a warm, heavy smell.
details: Depending on the wind, you catch it from two streets away. Some people hate it.
I don't mind. It smells like productivity to me.
reactions: You get used to the smell of your own settlement. It's everyone else's that seems strong.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the one building I'd hate to see damaged. We'd feel it immediately.
details: Everything else could be rebuilt slowly. The {poi_type} going down would stop daily life in its tracks.
I think about that sometimes when the weather turns bad.
reactions: Probably why people maintain it better than their own houses.

entries.intro: Someone planted herbs along the south wall of the {poi_type}. They're thriving.
details: Rosemary, thyme, something purple I can't name. The whole area smells wonderful now.
Whoever did it didn't ask permission. Nobody's complained.
reactions: The best improvements are the ones nobody argues about.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} runs on routine: same people, same hours, same output. Boring, but reliable.
details: I've never seen a day where it didn't open on time. Not once.
There's something comforting about that. You always know where you stand.
reactions: Reliability isn't exciting, but it's the thing you miss most when it's gone.

entries.intro: The sign on the {poi_type} has faded so much you can barely read it anymore.
details: The paint's peeled down to bare wood. Looks like nobody's touched it in years.
Everyone knows what it is by now, so I suppose a sign is just for visitors.
reactions: Still, a fresh coat of paint wouldn't kill anyone.

entries.intro: I used to work at the {poi_type}. Miss it sometimes, but my back doesn't.
details: It's honest work, but hard on the body. You feel every year of it in your joints.
The people there now are younger and faster. Good for them.
reactions: I did my time there and I'm glad I did. Wouldn't trade the experience, but I'm glad it's behind me.

entries.intro: A cat has claimed the {poi_type} as its territory. Sits on the windowsill and judges everyone.
details: It hisses at strangers and purrs at regulars. Better security than any lock.
The workers give it scraps. I think it's the real boss of that operation.
reactions: Every workplace needs a cat. Keeps the mice and the morale in check.

entries.intro: Water was pooling inside the {poi_type} after the last storm. They were bailing buckets for hours.
details: The drainage can't handle heavy downpours. Everything backs up and floods the lower area.
Lost some {resource_type} too. Anything stored on the floor was ruined.
reactions: They'll fix it after the next flood, and the one after that. We've been through this before.

statCheck.pass: The grade around the building slopes inward instead of away. Regrading the first two paces out from the wall would solve it permanently.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand drainage. I just know water goes where it wants.

entries.intro: The light inside the {poi_type} is always warm. Even on grey days it looks inviting from outside.
details: I think they use tallow candles or fat lamps. Whatever it is, it casts a golden glow.
On cold evenings you can see it through the windows and it makes you want to go in.
reactions: A well-lit space feels alive. A dark one feels abandoned even if it's not.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} goes through {resource_type} faster than you'd expect. The consumption is staggering.
details: I helped with an inventory count once and the numbers shocked me. Mountains of material in, a trickle out.
Most of it's processing loss. Waste is just the cost of the trade.
reactions: Makes you appreciate what comes out the other end.

statCheck.pass: Yield ratios depend on the quality of the raw input. Better starting material means less waste, but it costs more up front: always a tradeoff.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand the process. I just know a lot goes in and less comes out.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is where all the arguments happen. Something about that place draws disagreements.
details: I think it's because everyone has an opinion about how things should be done there.
Two people will walk in friendly and come out red-faced. Happens at least once a week.
reactions: Passion about the work, I suppose. Better than nobody caring.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is starting to look its age. Nothing's falling down, but you can see the wear.
details: Scuff marks on the doorframe, grooves worn into the floor, stains that won't come out.
It's the kind of aging that comes from heavy use. Not neglect, just life.
reactions: A place that shows its use is a place that matters. Pristine means unused.

entries.intro: I heard singing coming from the {poi_type} the other day. Someone in there has a nice voice.
details: It was faint, just carrying on the breeze. One of the workers humming while they went about it.
I couldn't place the tune, but it had a steady rhythm. The kind of song you'd sing to match your hands.
reactions: People who sing while they work are doing all right.

entries.intro: There's a drainage ditch near the {poi_type} that hasn't been cleaned in ages. It's backing up.
details: Leaves, muck, bits of broken tools. Everything ends up in there.
When it overflows it sends dirty water right across the path.
reactions: I'd do it myself but nobody asked me, and volunteering for ditch cleaning is a slippery slope.

entries.intro: The view from the {poi_type} toward the {direction} is lovely on a clear day.
details: You can see the tree line and the hills beyond. Sometimes hawks circling up high.
I think the workers there don't even notice anymore. Too busy looking at their hands.
reactions: Wasted on the people who work there. They should charge admission for the view.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has been open since before most of us were born. Hard to imagine this place without it.
details: My parents talked about it the same way I do. Same building, same purpose, different hands.
The walls have heard more conversations than any person alive.
reactions: Some things should just keep going. The {poi_type} is one of them.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had rats last month. Took a week to sort it out.
details: They got into the stored {resource_type} and ruined a good portion of it.
The crew there was furious. Can't blame them: that's a lot of work eaten by vermin.
reactions: It happens. Doesn't make it any less frustrating.

statCheck.pass: Rats follow the food supply: if the storage wasn't sealed properly, it's an open invitation. Clay-lined bins with weighted lids would solve it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know rat behavior. I just know they ate our stuff.

entries.intro: I like the {poi_type} in the early morning before anyone else arrives. It's calm.
details: The door's usually unlocked, the tools are all in their places, everything waiting to be used.
There's a peacefulness to a workspace before the work begins.
reactions: I'd never tell the workers I sneak in early just to enjoy the quiet. They'd think I was strange.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the one building everyone can find with their eyes closed. It's that central.
details: You could lead a blindfolded person there by sound and smell alone.
Every direction in town is described relative to it. Left of the {poi_type}, past the {poi_type}, behind the {poi_type}.
reactions: It's not the fanciest building. It's just the one that matters.

entries.intro: Birds nest in the eaves of the {poi_type} every spring. Makes a terrible mess.
details: Droppings on the entrance stones, straw falling from above, chirping at dawn.
The workers complained last year but nobody wanted to be the one to knock the nests down.
reactions: They'll be gone by autumn. Easier to wait than to fight nature.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has been surprisingly quiet this week. Less output, fewer people around.
details: I don't know if it's planned or if something's wrong. Nobody's said anything.
It's unsettling when a busy place goes still.
reactions: Probably just a slow stretch. Doesn't stop me from worrying, though.

entries.intro: Frost covered the {poi_type} this morning. Every surface was white and glittering.
details: The workers were scraping ice off their tools before they could start. Cold hands and stiff joints.
It was beautiful to look at, though. Like the building had been sugar-coated.
reactions: Pretty for me. Miserable for the people who have to work there.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} draws the best workers in the settlement. People want to be assigned there.
details: It's not the easiest post, but the people who run it treat their crew fairly.
I've heard folks turn down other offers just to stay on at the {poi_type}. That says something.
reactions: Good leadership pulls good people. It's that simple.

entries.intro: I swear the {poi_type} is louder on certain days. Maybe it depends on the wind direction.
details: Some mornings I can hear every clang and shout. Other days it's barely a murmur.
Wind from the {direction} seems to carry the sound right to my door.
reactions: Not much I can do about wind. Just close the shutters and deal with it.

statCheck.pass: Sound carries farther in cold, dense air, and a shift in wind direction changes which parts of town get the noise. Just physics.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why some days are louder. It just seems random.

entries.intro: Someone stacked firewood along the whole back wall of the {poi_type}. Looks like they're expecting a long winter.
details: It's piled higher than the windows. Must have taken days to cut and haul all that.
Either they know something about the weather that I don't, or they're just thorough.
reactions: Better too much than too little. You don't want to be scrounging for fuel in a cold snap.

entries.intro: I like the {poi_type} in autumn best. The color of the trees around it, the cooler air.
details: Everything looks warmer somehow, even though the temperature is dropping.
The smoke from the chimney stands out sharper against the blue sky.
reactions: Autumn makes everything look the way I feel about this place.

entries.intro: I noticed the {poi_type} left its doors open all night last week. Anyone could have walked in.
details: I didn't go in or anything. Just saw it on my way home and thought that's careless.
Nothing went missing, apparently. But it could have.
reactions: We're lucky we live in a place where an open door doesn't mean disaster. Not everywhere is like that.

entries.intro: The people at the {poi_type} have been working weekends. Whatever they're rushing for, it's urgent.
details: They look tired: short tempers, not much chatting. Just heads down and working.
I asked {npc_name} what was going on and they just shrugged. Tight-lipped about it.
reactions: If it were bad news, we'd know. Probably just a big order to fill.

entries.intro: The steps at the {poi_type} are worn smooth from years of foot traffic. Almost slippery.
details: The stone has dipped in the center where everyone walks. You can feel the groove.
That's thousands of people's footsteps carved into rock. There's something to that.
reactions: I always step to the side. Force of habit.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} was the first place I visited when I moved here. First impression of the whole settlement.
details: I remember thinking: if this place takes care of its {poi_type}, it takes care of its people.
The workers noticed me staring and waved me over. That kind of welcome sticks with you.
reactions: You can tell a lot about a community by the state of its working buildings.

entries.intro: There's a leak in the {poi_type}'s water supply: small but steady. You can see the damp patch spreading.
details: They've been ignoring it because it's not gushing. But slow leaks cause the most damage.
The wood around the wet spot is starting to darken. That's rot waiting to happen.
reactions: Fix it now or rebuild later. That's always the choice with water damage.

statCheck.pass: Water follows gravity and the path of least resistance. That damp patch means the leak source is higher and further back than where you see the stain.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand plumbing. I just see the wet spot getting bigger.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} always has the first fire lit in the morning. I can see the smoke from my window.
details: I use it as my alarm, honestly. Smoke rising means it's time to get moving.
Whoever starts that fire is up before the sun. Dedicated, or just can't sleep.
reactions: There's comfort in knowing the day has started somewhere, even when you're not ready for it.

entries.intro: A fight broke out near the {poi_type} yesterday. Nothing serious, just two people disagreeing loudly.
details: Something about who was responsible for a missed delivery. Voices carried across the whole area.
It blew over fast. They were sharing a drink by evening.
reactions: That's how it goes. You argue, you sort it, you move on.

entries.intro: The ground near the {poi_type} freezes before anywhere else in town. Something about the shade.
details: The building blocks the morning sun, so the frost lingers there until almost noon.
I've slipped on that patch twice this year already.
reactions: You'd think I'd learn. But every year I forget until I'm on the ground.

statCheck.pass: North-facing ground in building shadow gets no direct sun from autumn through early spring. The soil there never fully thaws in winter.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it's always icy there. It just is.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} produces enough {resource_type} to keep us comfortable. Not rich, but comfortable.
details: There's always enough to go around, with a little left over for trade.
Nobody's going without, and nobody's hoarding. The balance feels right.
reactions: Comfortable is underrated. People always want more, but comfortable is plenty.

entries.intro: I tried explaining to a traveler where the {poi_type} was. They walked right past it twice.
details: It doesn't look like much from the road. You have to know what you're looking for.
I ended up just walking them there. Easier than giving directions.
reactions: We know every stone of this place. Easy to forget that nobody else does.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} could really use a coat of whitewash. It's looking drab.
details: The color's gone from warm cream to grey-brown. Just from weather and time.
It wouldn't take long. A day's work, maybe two.
reactions: Appearances matter more than people think. A clean-looking building lifts the whole area.

entries.intro: I dropped off some food at the {poi_type} for the workers. They seemed grateful.
details: They don't always remember to eat, especially when they're focused on a big job.
A warm meal in the middle of a long shift does more than people realize.
reactions: Small gestures. That's how a settlement holds together.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a loose shutter that bangs every time the wind blows. It's maddening if you're nearby.
details: Bang, pause, bang: all day, every windy day.
I counted once: fourteen bangs in a minute before I gave up.
reactions: A nail and a hammer. That's literally all it would take.

entries.intro: The afternoon shadow of the {poi_type} stretches all the way across the square. Like a sundial.
details: You can tell the time by where the shadow's edge falls. I do it without thinking.
When it reaches the well, it's mid-afternoon. When it hits the houses, supper's close.
reactions: People had clocks before they had clocks. You just use what's around you.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} was extra productive this month. People are in good spirits about it.
details: More {resource_type} than we've seen in a long time. Surplus, even.
When there's enough to share, the whole settlement feels lighter.
reactions: Months like this make the lean ones worth pushing through.

entries.intro: I heard {npc_name} complaining about the {poi_type} again. They're never satisfied.
details: Always something: the hours, the quality, the noise, the location. Pick a gripe, they've got it.
Meanwhile, the rest of us are getting on fine.
reactions: Some people just need something to be unhappy about.

entries.intro: I helped repair a hinge at the {poi_type} yesterday. Small job, felt good to contribute.
details: The door had been sticking for weeks. Ten minutes with a file and some grease and it swings smooth.
The workers acted like I'd rebuilt the whole building.
reactions: That's the kind of thing I can actually help with. I'll leave the hard labor to others.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is getting cluttered. Supplies stacked everywhere, no clear walkway.
details: Used to be organized. Now it looks like everything just gets dropped wherever there's space.
Sooner or later someone's going to trip over something and get hurt.
reactions: A messy workspace is a slow workspace. But nobody asked me.

entries.intro: Spring runoff flows right past the {poi_type}. You can hear the water rushing from the path.
details: It only happens for a few weeks, but when it does the whole area sounds like a creek.
The ground goes soft and the earthworms surface. The birds follow.
reactions: I look forward to it every year. The sound of meltwater means winter's done.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s output has been inconsistent. Good one week, poor the next.
details: You can't plan around that. People need to know what they're going to get.
I think the problem is they're trying new methods and not sticking to what works.
reactions: Experimentation is fine, but not when people are depending on you.

statCheck.pass: Inconsistency usually comes from variable input quality: if the raw {resource_type} varies batch to batch, the output will too. They need a grading step at intake.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the trade well enough to diagnose it. Just that the results keep changing.

entries.intro: There's a cistern near the {poi_type} that collects rainwater. It's been full all season.
details: Clean water, always available. It saves trips to the well, especially for the workers.
Someone rigged a clever lid that keeps the debris out but lets the rain in.
reactions: Simple solutions are the best ones. Nobody argues with free water.

entries.intro: I miss how the {poi_type} used to run. It felt more organized, more purposeful.
details: I can't point to a single thing that changed. It's more of a feeling.
The work still gets done. It just seems like more effort for the same result.
reactions: Could be me, could be the place. Probably a bit of both.

entries.intro: The chimney at the {poi_type} sends up the most beautiful smoke on cold mornings. Straight and white.
details: Against a blue winter sky it's like a column of cotton. I never get tired of seeing it.
You can tell the temperature by the smoke. The colder it is, the straighter it rises.
reactions: Simple pleasure, watching smoke in still air.

statCheck.pass: Cold air is denser, so smoke rises faster and straighter. Wind shear from temperature differences causes it to bend on warmer days.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why smoke behaves differently. It just does.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is too dark inside. I don't know how the workers see what they're doing.
details: Two small windows and a doorway. That's all the light they get.
I went in at noon and still had to wait for my eyes to adjust.
reactions: A couple of skylights would change everything. But that means cutting through the roof.

statCheck.pass: South-facing clerestory windows would bring in natural light without compromising the roof structure. The rafters have enough spacing for it.
statCheck.fail: I'm no architect. I just know it's gloomy in there.

entries.intro: Every season the {poi_type} looks a little different. The ivy grows, the wood weathers, the path shifts.
details: If I compared it to five years ago I'd barely recognize the details, even though the building hasn't changed.
It's like watching someone age. The shape stays the same but everything softens.
reactions: I find it comforting: nothing stays still, and that's not a bad thing.

entries.intro: I offered to bring {resource_type} to the {poi_type} and they turned me down. Said they were full up.
details: First time I can remember them refusing a delivery. Storage must be packed.
I peeked inside and every shelf was stacked. {resource_type} floor to ceiling.
reactions: Better to have too much than too little.

entries.intro: A section of the path to the {poi_type} collapsed after the rains. Whole edge just slid away.
details: It's still passable if you keep to the far side, but it's narrower now.
Nobody's done anything about it. We all just walk around the gap like it's normal.
reactions: Give it another storm and the whole path will be gone. But sure, let's wait.

statCheck.pass: The soil there is undermined by water runoff from the slope above. Without a retaining wall, each rain will take more of the edge.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it collapsed. The rain, probably.

entries.intro: There's a wildflower that only grows near the {poi_type}. I've never seen it anywhere else around here.
details: Small blue thing, clusters of five petals. It comes up in late spring between the stones.
I asked around and nobody knows what it's called.
reactions: Nice to have something that makes a spot feel unique.

statCheck.pass: Certain plants thrive in disturbed soil with specific mineral content. The runoff from the {poi_type} probably creates exactly the conditions it needs.
statCheck.fail: I don't know anything about botany. I just think it's pretty.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a reputation outside our settlement now. People from the {direction} come specifically for it.
details: The {resource_type} quality has spread by word of mouth. You see unfamiliar faces asking directions.
It's flattering, in a way. We built something worth traveling for.
reactions: Good problem to have. As long as the demand doesn't outstrip what we can produce.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} always falls quiet right before a storm. Like it knows what's coming.
details: The workers pack up faster, the animals nearby get restless, and then everything goes still.
Twenty minutes later the sky opens. Every time.
reactions: I trust the {poi_type}'s instinct more than I trust the sky's color.

statCheck.pass: Animals sense the pressure drop before a storm front. The workers there read the same signs: wind shift, temperature change, the way sound carries differently in dense air.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how they always know. I just know they're never wrong.

entries.intro: The storage area at the {poi_type} is overflowing. There's {resource_type} stacked outside under tarps.
details: It's been like that for weeks. They've outgrown the space.
Rain got under the covering last time and damaged some of the stock. Preventable waste.
reactions: Either expand the storage or slow down production. Leaving it outside is asking for trouble.

entries.intro: I sat with {npc_name} outside the {poi_type} last evening. Just talked about nothing for an hour.
details: The work was done, the sky was clear, and neither of us had anywhere to be.
We watched the stars come out over the roof. Didn't need to say much.
reactions: That's what a good place does. It gives people a reason to linger.

entries.intro: There are boot prints worn into the stone at the {poi_type}'s threshold. Decades of the same footsteps.
details: Right foot, then left, in and out. Thousands of times until the stone remembers.
You don't notice it until someone points it out. Then you can't unsee it.
reactions: That's not wear and tear. That's history.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} wasted a whole day's {resource_type} on a botched job last week. People are annoyed.
details: The material was fine going in. Something went wrong during processing and it all had to be scrapped.
In a settlement this size, a full day's loss hurts.
reactions: Mistakes happen. But that's a lot of work gone for nothing.

statCheck.pass: From what I heard, the temperature wasn't regulated properly. Too hot or too cold by a narrow margin and the whole batch is ruined.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand the process well enough to say what went wrong.

entries.intro: I saw a hawk perched on top of the {poi_type} this morning. Just sitting there surveying everything.
details: It must like the height. Best vantage point in the settlement.
The workers came out and it didn't move. Just stared at them and they stared back.
reactions: Even the wildlife knows the {poi_type} is the center of things.

entries.intro: The smell of {crop_type} drying near the {poi_type} fills the whole street. Rich and sweet.
details: They hang it on racks in the sun and by afternoon the aroma drifts halfway across town.
I find myself taking the long way just to walk through it.
reactions: There are worse problems than a settlement that smells like a harvest.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} lost a worker last month. They haven't replaced them yet.
details: Not dead, just moved on. Found something better, I suppose.
The ones left are covering the gap. They're managing, but only just.
reactions: People leaving is always harder on the ones who stay.

entries.intro: The door at the {poi_type} has marks on the frame where they measure the harvest each year. Goes back ages.
details: Some years the marks are high, some are barely there. You can read the settlement's history in those scratches.
The best years have deep, confident notches. The lean years are faint, like nobody wanted to record them.
reactions: We're adding to that record whether we think about it or not.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} creaks in the heat. The timbers expand and the whole building groans.
details: In midsummer you can hear it popping and ticking all afternoon as the sun bakes the roof.
The workers say they tune it out. I don't know how.
reactions: Buildings talk. You just have to learn the language.

statCheck.pass: Timber expands and contracts with temperature, and the joints flex: that's what you hear. It's actually a sign the structure is well-built, since rigid framing would crack instead.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why buildings make noise. The heat, I assume.

entries.intro: I brought my child to see the {poi_type} for the first time last week. They were fascinated.
details: Asked a hundred questions about what everything was for. The workers were patient about it.
Seeing it through a child's eyes made me realize how remarkable the everyday really is.
reactions: We forget to be impressed by things we see every day.

entries.intro: Ants have built a nest under the foundation of the {poi_type}. Huge colony, sand piling up everywhere.
details: You can see the mounds along the base of the wall. Must be thousands of them.
Nobody's sure if they're actually damaging anything or just living there.
reactions: Tiny tenants. Probably been there longer than we have.

statCheck.pass: If they're excavating under the footing, they can undermine the load-bearing capacity over time. Small creatures, big consequences if left unchecked.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if ants can hurt a building. Seems unlikely, but what do I know.

entries.intro: The new crew at the {poi_type} is faster than the last one. You can see the difference in the output.
details: They're younger, stronger, and they work like they've got something to prove.
The old-timers grumble about it, but the results speak for themselves.
reactions: Fresh energy is exactly what that place needed.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} always gets a breeze from the {direction}. Even on still days there's air moving through.
details: In summer it's a blessing: keeps the air moving and the heat bearable. In winter it cuts right through you.
The building's positioned right in a natural wind channel. Nothing to be done about it.
reactions: I suppose the original builders either didn't notice or didn't care.

statCheck.pass: The gap between the hillside and the ridge funnels prevailing winds directly through that area. It's a topographic effect: the land shapes the airflow.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it's always breezy there. Just the way it is.

entries.intro: I dream about the {poi_type} sometimes. Just standing there, nobody else around.
details: It's always the same: the building's there, the light's wrong, and I feel like I'm supposed to do something.
I wake up and nothing's different: just a building. But the feeling lingers.
reactions: You spend enough time near a place, it follows you home.

entries.intro: The {resource_type} coming out of the {poi_type} has a reputation. People ask for it by name.
details: There's something about the process there that gives it an edge over what you'd get elsewhere.
I couldn't tell you the difference myself, but people who know swear by it.
reactions: Reputation is earned slowly and lost fast. They've earned theirs.

entries.intro: I keep finding nails on the path near the {poi_type}. Someone's dropping them.
details: Third time this month I've stepped on one. Didn't go through my boot, but it could have.
I've started collecting them in a jar. Got quite the pile now.
reactions: I should return them. Or just keep collecting and see how many I get by winter.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the warmest building in the settlement during winter. People find excuses to go in.
details: The fires they keep for work throw off enough heat to warm the whole place.
I've seen people linger near the entrance just to catch the warmth drifting out.
reactions: Can't blame them. I've done the same.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has moss growing on the north wall. Thick and green, like velvet.
details: It's been creeping up for years. Now it covers the whole face from ground to window.
Some people think it looks neglected. I think it looks natural.
reactions: A building that the forest wants to reclaim is a building in the right place.

statCheck.pass: Moss holds moisture against the stone, which accelerates weathering. It looks pretty, but it's slowly eating the mortar.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if moss damages stone. It seems harmless.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the last place to go dark at night and the first to light up in the morning.
details: You can track the workday by its windows: lit means someone's there, dark means rest.
I find it reassuring, seeing that glow when I can't sleep. Like a heartbeat for the settlement.
reactions: As long as the lights keep coming on, we're doing all right.
